TableReference: *Normal range and (mean). †Mean and (98th percentile). Data from Park MK. Pediatric Cardiology for Practitioners. 5th ed. St Louis: Elsevier; 2008 and Davignon A, et al. Normal ECG standards for infants and children. Pediatr Cardiol. 1979;1:123-131.

TableInstruction: +, T wave positive; −, T wave negative; ±, T wave normally either positive or negative

Requirement: Must Have at Least One of the Following:
Criteria: Upright T wave in lead V₁ after 3 days of age to adolescence
Presence of Q wave in V₁ (QR or QRS pattern)
Increased right and anterior QRS voltage (with normal QRS duration): R in lead V₁, >98th percentile for age; S in lead V₆, >98th percentile for age
Right ventricular strain (associated with inverted T wave in V₁ with tall R wave)
Left ventricular strain (associated with inverted T wave in leads V₆, I, and/or aVF)
Left axis deviation (LAD) for patient's age
Volume overload (associated with Q wave >5 mm and tall T waves in V₅ or V₆)
Increased QRS voltage in left leads (with normal QRS duration): R in lead V₆ (and I, aVL, V₅), >98th percentile for age; S in lead V₁, >98th percentile for age

TableName: MAJOR SYNDROMES ASSOCIATED WITH CARDIAC DEFECTS
Dominant Cardiac Defect: TOF, truncus arteriosus, aortic arch abnormalities
Aortic arch anomalies, TOF, truncus arteriosus, VSD, PDA
Aortic root dilation, mitral valve prolapse
Aortic root dilation with higher risk of rupture at smaller dimensions
COA, bicuspid aortic valve, aortic root dilation as a teenager
Supravalvular aortic stenosis, pulmonary artery stenosis
Truncus arteriosus, TOF, pulmonary atresia with VSD, TGA, interrupted aortic arch

TableName: CARDIAC CONDITIONS FOR WHICH ANTIBIOTIC PROPHYLAXIS IS RECOMMENDED FOR DENTAL, RESPIRATORY TRACT, INFECTED SKIN, SKIN STRUCTURES, OR MUSCULOSKELETAL TISSUE PROCEDURES

Conditions.MainCondition: Congenital heart disease (CHD)—Limited to the following conditions¹
SubConditions: Unrepaired cyanotic defect, including palliative shunts and conduits
Completely repaired CHD with prosthetic material/device (placed by surgery or catheterization), during first 6 months after procedure²
Repaired CHD with residual defects at or adjacent to the site of prosthetic patch or device (which inhibit endothelialization)

Conditions: Cardiac transplantation patients who develop cardiac valvulopathy

TableName: PROPHYLACTIC REGIMENS FOR DENTAL AND RESPIRATORY TRACT PROCEDURES
GUIDELINES FOR DIAGNOSIS OF INITIAL ATTACK OF RHEUMATIC FEVER (JONES CRITERIA)

Laboratory findings: Elevated acute phase reactants (erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein)

Supporting Evidence of Antecedent Group: Positive throat culture or rapid streptococcal antigen test
Elevated or rising streptococcal antibody titer
TableInstruction: NOTE: If supported by evidence of preceding group A streptococcal infection, the presence of two major manifestations or of one major and two minor manifestations indicates a high probability of acute rheumatic fever.

TableName: ELECTROLYTE COMPOSITION OF VARIOUS BODY FLUIDS

Source: Liver, heart, skeletal muscle, pancreas, RBCs, kidney
Increased: Hepatocellular injury, rhabdomyolysis, muscular dystrophy, hemolysis, liver cancer
Comments: ALT more specific than AST for liver, AST > ALT in hemolysis, AST/ALT >2 in 90% of alcohol disorders in adults

Source: Osteoblasts, liver, small intestine, kidney, placenta
Increased: Hepatocellular injury, bone growth, disease, trauma, pregnancy, familial
Decreased: Low phosphate, Wilson disease, zinc deficiency, hypothyroidism, pernicious anemia
Comments: Highest in cholestatic conditions; must be differentiated from bone source

Source: Renal tubules, bile ducts, pancreas, small intestine, brain
Increased: Cholestasis, newborn period, induced by drugs
Decreased: Estrogen therapy, artificially low in hyperbilirubinemia
Comments: Not found in bone, increased in 90% of primary liver disease, specific for hepatobiliary disease in nonpregnant patient

Source: Intestine, liver cell membrane, brain, heart, pancreas
Comments: Specific for hepatobiliary disease in nonpregnant patient

Increased: Hepatic disease secondary to urea cycle dysfunction, hemodialysis, valproic acid therapy, urea cycle enzyme deficiency, organic acidemia and carnitine deficiency
TableInstruction: AST/ALT, Aspartate aminotransferase/alanine aminotransferase; 5'–NT, 5'–nucleotidase; GGT, γ-glutamyl transpeptidase; RBCs, red blood cells

TableInstruction: Heparinized venous specimens on ice, analyzed within 30 min.
Patterns with clinical correlation: Centromere: CREST; Nucleolar: Scleroderma; Homogeneous: Systemic lupus erythematosus.
Fourfold rise in paired serial specimens is significant.
Refer to neonatal hyperbilirubinemia guidelines for clinical interpretation.
Venous blood gases assess acid-base status, not oxygenation. Peripheral venous samples correlate best with arterial pH; capillary samples correlate moderately with PaCO2.
